title: Validation and error recovery

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Validation and error recovery; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Validation and error recovery
sources.claim: React form validation combines native constraints, controlled domain rules, submit handling, accessible errors, and authoritative server results.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Validation and error recovery
sources.claim: React form validation combines native constraints, controlled domain rules, submit handling, accessible errors, and authoritative server results.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React form validation combines native constraints, controlled domain rules, submit handling, accessible errors, and authoritative server results.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Submit reveals linked text errors, focuses the first invalid field, and preserves server error messages safely. Your goal is to implement and test a form's invalid, submitting, success and server-error states. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State and events; html forms; Controlled inputs; html forms validation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React form validation combines native constraints, controlled domain rules, submit handling, accessible errors, and authoritative server results. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Validation timing can annoy users, async checks race, and client rules remain bypassable. This draft therefore rejects: Disabling the submit button is enough to explain every invalid field.

includes: Combine native constraints and domain validation with persistent instructions and focus-aware errors.
Implement and test a form's invalid, submitting, success and server-error states.
excludes: Validation timing can annoy users, async checks race, and client rules remain bypassable.

outcomes: Implement and test a form's invalid, submitting, success and server-error states.

examples.title: Validation and error recovery: concrete case
examples.situation: Submit reveals linked text errors, focuses the first invalid field, and preserves server error messages safely.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Submit reveals linked text errors, focuses the first invalid field, and preserves server error messages safely.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Expose field-specific text errors and server validation; disabled submission alone does not identify what to fix.
examples.result: The result follows because Expose field-specific text errors and server validation; disabled submission alone does not identify what to fix.

examples.title: Validation and error recovery: boundary case
examples.situation: Validation timing can annoy users, async checks race, and client rules remain bypassable.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Validation timing can annoy users, async checks race, and client rules remain bypassable.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Disabling the submit button is enough to explain every invalid field.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Expose field-specific text errors and server validation; disabled submission alone does not identify what to fix.

limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
